answer.startPath: create a downstream business package or service
answer.reason: business capabilities are registered through public capability manifests and projected into platform contracts

businessCapability.registration: append CatalogManifest() in the downstream composition root before resolving enabled capabilities
businessCapability.replaceableExampleDomain: catalog is a placeholder domain; rename the capability, resources, routes, permissions, demo records and package path for a real business project

notes: The example lifecycle steps are no-op functions recorded through the public lifecycle executor.
The auth provider is declared but disabled; its config keys are contract metadata, not required local secrets.
The service surface is contract-only; downstream packages own handlers, persistence and event delivery.

commands.proves: static template, docs, Go manifest shape, go test and JSON preview
negative coverage for missing template, internal imports and incomplete tutorial metadata
standalone downstream Go module tests pass from the repository root
the manifest can produce a JSON-safe contract preview without external config

localTutorial.moduleBoundary: examples/external-capability is a standalone nested Go module. From the repository root use go -C or run inside the example directory instead of go test ./examples/external-capability/...

declares: admin resources with schema fields, menus, permission prefixes, actions, panels and runtime slots
app routes with auth mode and app permission

adminSurface.projection: capability.Manifest.Admin.Resources -> admin resource contract -> menu, permissions, schema and settings aggregation

configuration.configResourceDiscovery: GET /api/capabilities exposes catalog-settings in configResources after the downstream manifest is enabled and the service restarts

demoData.productionRule: demo data remains declarative and optional; production deployments must not rely on demo-data capability defaults

copyChecklist: Create a downstream Go module and require github.com/GnosiST/platform-go at a released version.
Copy the manifest pattern, then rename capability ID, package path, resource keys, routes, permission prefixes, config keys and demo data.
Keep imports limited to github.com/GnosiST/platform-go/pkg/platform/capability for manifest contracts.
Register the manifest in the downstream composition root before resolving enabled capabilities.
Keep handlers, repositories, migrations with side effects, custom Admin renderers and deployment wiring in the downstream project.
Regenerate contracts for the target capability set, rebuild the downstream artifact and restart the service.

deployment.package: build a downstream API binary or service image that includes the business capability manifest
deployment.enable: select platform capabilities plus example-catalog in the downstream desired capability list
deployment.restart: regenerate contracts and restart the API process; v1 does not hot-load external packages

upgradeStrategy.platform: upgrade the platform module version, run contract validators, then rebuild the downstream composition root
upgradeStrategy.business: bump the capability semver, keep resource keys and permission prefixes stable, add idempotent migrations for data changes
upgradeStrategy.rollback: roll back the downstream binary and desired capability set; persisted business data remains owned by the business package

mainlineIntegrationNotes.promoteToPlatformOnlyWhen: the behavior is proven reusable across products and has its own public contract, profile policy, validators, docs and migration evidence
mainlineIntegrationNotes.businessNeutrality: example-catalog is not a built-in platform capability and must remain replaceable sample material
